stem: What is the single invariant that governs all prompt caching behavior?
options: Caching applies to any request content, wherever it appears
Caching is an exact prefix match — the key is the rendered request up to a breakpoint, and a single changed byte invalidates everything after it
Caching matches semantically similar prompts
Caching applies per message, independently
rationales: Only the prefix up to a breakpoint is reusable — position matters totally.
This one rule explains every caching failure: most are not missing markers but unstable prefixes. A byte of drift anywhere kills everything downstream of it.
There is nothing semantic about it — byte equality or nothing.
The unit is the cumulative rendered prefix, not individual messages.

stem: In what order does a request render for cache-matching purposes, and why does changing the tool set mid-conversation hurt so much?
options: Messages, then system, then tools — tool changes only affect the tail
Order is configurable per request
Alphabetically by field name
Tools, then system prompt, then messages — tools render at position zero, so a tool set change invalidates the ENTIRE cached prefix (and caches are model-scoped, so model switches do the same)
rationales: Backwards — tools render first, making them the most invalidating thing to change.
Render order is fixed by the API.
It renders by structure, not alphabetically.
Position zero means maximum blast radius. "Modes" implemented by swapping tool sets are cache-hostile — pass the mode as message content instead.

stem: Which of these does NOT silently invalidate a cached prefix?
options: Serializing the tool list as JSON with sorted keys on every request
A "current date" string interpolated into the system prompt
Building the tool list from an unordered collection
A per-session ID early in the prompt
rationales: Deterministic serialization is the FIX, not the problem — same bytes every render.
The classic cache-killer: every request becomes a unique prefix.
Non-deterministic ordering means byte-different renders of identical content.
Session IDs early in the prompt prevent sharing across requests (and users).

stem: Which statement about cache breakpoints and TTLs is TRUE?
options: Unlimited breakpoints; entries last 24 hours
One breakpoint per request; entries never expire
Up to four breakpoints per request; default TTL is five minutes (one-hour option at higher write cost); prefixes below a model-dependent minimum size are silently not cached at all
Breakpoints are placed automatically by the API
rationales: Both numbers are wrong — small breakpoint budget, short default TTL.
Entries expire quickly by default; that's central to the economics.
The silent minimum is the trap: markers on a too-small prefix simply do nothing, with no error. Place breakpoints at stability boundaries — end of system prompt, most recent turn.
You place `cache_control` markers explicitly.

stem: How do you VERIFY caching is actually working, rather than assuming the markers do their job?
options: Watch overall latency — cached requests are visibly faster
Check the response usage block, which reports cache writes and cache reads separately from uncached input — zero reads across repeated identical-prefix requests means a silent invalidator
Trust the SDK to raise an error if caching fails
Compare monthly bills before and after
rationales: Latency varies for many reasons; it's an unreliable proxy.
Cache behavior is directly observable per request. The diagnostic when reads are zero: byte-diff two consecutive rendered requests to find the invalidator.
Cache misses are silent — no error is ever raised.
A monthly signal for a per-request mechanism is far too slow a feedback loop.

stem: What are the economics of prompt caching in round numbers?
options: Writes are free; reads cost double normal input
Both writes and reads cost more than normal input — caching pays off only at extreme volume
Reads are free forever once written
Writes cost slightly more than normal input; reads cost a small fraction of it — caching pays for itself by the second hit, and traffic arriving more often than the TTL keeps the cache warm by itself
rationales: Writes carry a modest premium — they aren't free.
Payback arrives at hit two, not at extreme volume.
Reads are dramatically cheaper (~an order of magnitude), not free, and entries expire.
Corollary: a prefix that never repeats gains nothing — markers on it only pay write premiums. Do not cache what never repeats.

stem: A conversation no longer fits in the context window. Someone suggests enabling prompt caching to fix it. What is the correct response?
options: Caching changes cost, not capacity — an over-long request still doesn't fit; the fixes are compaction, trimming, or summarization
Good idea — cached tokens don't count against the window
Good idea, but only with the one-hour TTL
It works if breakpoints are placed every thousand tokens
rationales: Wrong lever for the constraint. Cached input is cheaper to process, but it's still input in the window.
Cached tokens fully count against the context window.
TTL affects reuse economics, not capacity.
No breakpoint arrangement changes the window size.
sourceRef: ## 13 > When Caching Is the Answer (and When It Is Not)

stem: Match the cost lever to the actual constraint: repeated expensive prefix / deferrable volume / over-tiered capability / bloated unique content.
options: Trim / cache / batch / smaller model
Batch / cache / trim / smaller model

Cache / batch / smaller model / trim — each lever addresses exactly one kind of expense, and they compose (batched requests support caching; a cheaper model with a warm cache is often cheapest of all)
Smaller model for all four — capability is the only real cost
rationales: Trimming a repeated prefix loses content that caching would have made nearly free.
Batching doesn't reward repetition; caching doesn't reward deferrability.
The matching discipline is the exam skill: identify what is actually expensive, then pick the lever built for it. Composability means the answer is often "two of these together."
Model tier is one lever among four — it does nothing for a repeated prefix or deferrable volume.
sourceRef: ## 13 > When Caching Is the Answer (lever matching)

stem: A high-traffic cached agent needs per-user state (current plan, recent events) in context. Where does it go?
options: Interpolated into the system prompt, where state belongs
Injected late in the context — after the last breakpoint, near the latest turn — keeping the system prompt frozen so the shared prefix stays cacheable
In the tool definitions, which are always loaded
Nowhere — cached agents cannot carry per-user state
rationales: On a cached agent, rewriting the system prompt per user or per event re-processes the entire conversation uncached.
Order by stability: frozen tools and system prompt up front, volatile state after the last breakpoint. Users share the cached prefix; each request pays only for its own volatile tail.
Tool definitions render at position zero — the most cache-hostile location available.
State is fine; its position is what matters.

stem: Roughly how much cheaper is reading cached tokens versus processing them fresh?
options: About an order of magnitude cheaper, while cache writes carry a modest premium over normal input
About 10% cheaper
Exactly half price
Free
rationales: That scale is why caching often beats model choice and prompt trimming as the first cost lever for repeated-prefix workloads.
Far too small — the read discount is dramatic.
Half-off is the BATCH discount; don't mix the two levers' numbers.
Cheap, not free.
